subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Professional Degree (e.g., MD, JD)
Completed degree in a technical or engineering-related field, such as Mechanical Engineering or Industrial Design
Undertook specialized coursework in computer-aided design (CAD), technical drawing, and mechanical systems
Attended professional workshops on SolidWorks and AutoCAD during early career
Obtained certification in SolidWorks (CSWA) to validate CAD proficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Mechanical Drafter
Responsible for preparing detailed layouts and technical drawings for machinery, mechanical tools, and devices
Collaborates closely with engineers to translate conceptual designs into manufacturable schematics
Creates both 2D technical drawings and 3D CAD models
Ensures designs comply with industry standards and internal specifications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the manufacturing sector
Focuses on machinery and mechanical device design
Supports production of industrial equipment and precision tools
Collaborates with suppliers and manufacturing teams to ensure design feasibility

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized engineering firm with approximately 100-250 employees
Works within a dedicated design and drafting team of 6-10 people
Company has a collaborative, project-based work environment
Frequent cross-departmental interaction with engineering and manufacturing groups

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Company specializes in custom machinery and mechanical component design
Serves clients in industrial automation, manufacturing, and tooling
Projects often involve retrofitting existing systems or creating bespoke solutions
Market segment includes both domestic and international clients

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 6-10 years of experience as a Mechanical Drafter
Started as a junior drafter and advanced to lead complex projects
Has completed over 50 major drafting projects during tenure
Experience spans both 2D drafting and 3D modeling responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a mid-to-senior level drafter position
Often serves as lead drafter on project teams
Mentors junior or entry-level drafters on best practices
Contributes to process improvement discussions within the design team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Prepares detailed technical layouts using AutoCAD and SolidWorks
Translates engineering concepts and sketches into precise CAD models
Reviews and revises drawings based on engineer and client feedback
Collaborates with engineers to ensure manufacturability and compliance
Maintains drawing libraries and manages version control for designs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 70% of work hours on CAD drafting and modeling
About 15% of time is dedicated to meetings and collaborative sessions
10% involves reviewing, revising, and annotating existing drawings
The remaining time is spent on administrative tasks and documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with moderate autonomy in selecting drafting methods and tools
Responsible for technical accuracy and completeness of own drawings
Decisions on design details are made in consultation with engineers
Has authority to propose process improvements for drafting workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Creating basic 2D layouts from clear engineering sketches
Applying standard dimensioning and annotation to drawings
Using AutoCAD for repetitive geometric shapes and patterns
Updating drawing revisions based on minor changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Converting vague or incomplete concepts into manufacturable designs
Creating complex assemblies with multiple moving parts in SolidWorks
Ensuring drawings meet all regulatory and safety standards
Coordinating with multiple stakeholders to resolve conflicting requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Drafting standard components for recurring projects
Populating parts lists and bill of materials from CAD models
Applying repetitive dimensioning or tolerance annotations
Checking drawings for compliance against a fixed checklist

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys collaborating with engineers to solve unique design challenges
Finds satisfaction in building accurate 3D models from scratch
Considers repetitive dimensioning or data entry as tedious
Least enjoys extensive administrative documentation or file management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manually updating multiple drawing versions after design changes
Interoperability issues between different CAD software versions
Delays caused by unclear or incomplete input from engineers
Time-consuming data entry for bills of materials and parts lists

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Productivity is highest when working on challenging but clear design problems
Enjoyment increases with autonomy and creative input on projects
Repetitive or low-skill tasks lower overall engagement and motivation
Higher skill level allows for faster completion of complex layouts, boosting job satisfaction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and enthusiastic about adopting new drafting technologies
Regularly seeks out updates and new features in CAD software
Views technology adoption as essential for staying competitive
Encourages team members to experiment with new digital tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self highly adaptable to new CAD platforms or plugins
Can learn and integrate new software features within a few days
Comfortable switching between AutoCAD and SolidWorks as needed
Has successfully adapted to LLM-based drafting assistants in daily workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Allocates time each month to explore new tutorials and webinars
Participates in company-sponsored training sessions
Willing to invest personal time in mastering tools that improve efficiency
Has completed multiple online courses for advanced CAD techniques

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by efficiency gains and professional growth
Occasional barrier: high workload limits time for deep learning
Prefers tech training directly relevant to current projects
Peer recommendations increase motivation to adopt new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Positive peer experiences accelerate adoption of new technologies
Management endorsement is key for company-wide tool adoption
Often acts as an early adopter, sharing findings with colleagues
Team culture encourages experimentation with new software

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses AutoCAD for 2D drafting tasks
Relies on SolidWorks for 3D modeling and assemblies
Employs Microsoft Excel for managing parts lists and project tracking
Incorporates LLM-based drafting assistants for automating annotation and documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses a workstation with a high-resolution dual monitor setup
Employs digital drawing tablets for sketching concepts
References printed and digital engineering standards manuals
Utilizes a networked file storage system for version control

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Advanced user of both AutoCAD and SolidWorks
Familiar with PDM (Product Data Management) systems
Knowledgeable about interoperability between major CAD platforms
Regularly updates skills as new versions of key software are released

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces issues transferring files between AutoCAD and SolidWorks
Some legacy files require manual conversion for compatibility
Integration between CAD and Excel for BOMs can be cumbersome
Company is piloting cloud-based solutions to improve cross-platform collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands key terms such as machine learning, natural language processing, and large language models (LLMs)
Regularly reads industry blogs and documentation on AI developments
Comfortable discussing AI model training, inference, and prompt engineering
Has experimented with generative design tools that use AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT for drafting technical documentation and answering design queries
Employs LLM plugins integrated with CAD software for automating annotations
Tests AI-powered generative design features in SolidWorks
Utilizes AI-based error-checking tools for CAD models

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools daily for technical writing and drawing annotation
Applies LLMs to automate repetitive documentation and parts list generation
Occasionally uses AI to suggest design optimizations based on project parameters
Employs AI chatbots as a reference for industry standards and best practices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used AI to reduce drafting time by automating standard annotations
Encountered issues with AI-generated parts lists missing non-standard components
Learned the importance of manual review after AI-assisted drafting
Found AI chatbots useful for quick reference but less reliable for complex design logic

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Company provides periodic webinars on AI integration with CAD tools
Peers share tips and workflows for maximizing AI tool efficiency
Documentation and guides for AI plugins are available on the company intranet
Encouraged to participate in external online courses on AI in engineering

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools for complex tasks
Uses independent, command-based interactions for routine drafting functions
Finds value in conversational AI for brainstorming design alternatives
Adapts interaction style depending on task complexity and urgency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant that augments routine work
Occasionally treats AI as a co-worker for brainstorming or ideation
Relies on AI for rapid information retrieval and error-checking
Maintains final decision-making authority over all outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to experimenting with new AI-driven drafting workflows
Actively participates in pilot programs for AI tool integration
Encourages team adoption of AI when demonstrable productivity gains are evident
Provides feedback to vendors on improving AI workflow features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for design ideation and technical documentation
Uses command-based interfaces for repetitive or batch operations
Finds hybrid interfaces (conversational + command) most effective for complex tasks
Values interfaces that allow easy context switching between modes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks require deep knowledge of mechanical engineering principles
Interpreting ambiguous client requirements depends on domain expertise
Designing for manufacturability involves tacit understanding of production constraints
AI tools currently lack nuanced understanding of certain industry-specific practices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine documentation and annotation tasks
Manually reviews all AI-generated outputs before submission
Skeptical of AI-generated designs for safety-critical or regulatory-compliant components
Trust increases with transparent AI decision-making and explainability

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers high AI automation for repetitive drafting and documentation
Maintains human oversight for design interpretation and problem-solving
Sees value in semi-automated workflows where AI assists but does not replace human judgment
Advocates for human-AI collaboration rather than full automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for standardized, low-risk tasks
Considers automation threatening if it undermines quality control or safety
Requires manual override and review capabilities in all automated workflows
Supports automation when it frees time for higher-value design activities

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the importance of deep domain expertise
Routine drafting skills are becoming less critical due to automation
Problem-solving and creative design skills are more valued in AI-augmented workflows
Continuous learning is essential to adapt to evolving AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing prompt engineering and AI workflow customization skills
Responsible for validating and curating AI-generated outputs
Learning to troubleshoot and adapt AI tools for domain-specific tasks
Participates in evaluating and recommending new AI solutions for the team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about automation of routine drafting reducing task variety
Confident that creative and interpretive aspects of drafting remain human-centric
Monitors industry trends regarding AI-driven job displacement
Actively seeks opportunities to upskill and adapt to changing demands

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-assisted outputs are subject to manual review before approval
CAD plugins allow users to accept, reject, or modify AI-generated suggestions
Company policy mandates human sign-off for all critical design documents
Regularly audits AI workflows to ensure compliance and accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has formalized guidelines for responsible AI tool use
Recent policy updates encourage experimentation with AI for efficiency gains
Team is expected to document and share best practices for AI integration
Ongoing discussions on standardizing AI tool evaluation and procurement

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will significantly increase productivity in manufacturing and engineering
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to advanced design tools
Concerned about possible job displacement for low-skill roles
Optimistic that AI will drive innovation and new business models in engineering

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent and explainable AI systems to build user trust
Believes in the necessity of human oversight for all safety-critical tasks
Worries about data privacy and intellectual property risks with cloud-based AI
Advocates for equitable access to AI training and upskilling within the team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools with better context awareness for complex assemblies
Seeks improved integration between LLMs and CAD platforms
Wishes for AI-powered real-time error detection during drafting
Would benefit from AI that can auto-generate compliance documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates a shift toward more design validation and creative problem-solving
Expects routine drafting tasks to become fully automated
Foresees increasing collaboration with AI to explore innovative design concepts
Believes new roles will emerge focused on AI tool management and optimization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Provide ongoing training and resources for AI literacy and upskilling
Establish clear guidelines for ethical and responsible AI use
Invest in robust integration of AI with core engineering tools
Encourage open feedback and iterative improvement of AI workflows
